page: Google Cloud Platform c4n-highcpu-2 is a compute-optimized virtual machine belonging to the c4n server family. It is configured with 2 dedicated vCPUs based on the x86_64 CPU architecture, paired with 4.0 GB of system memory. This configuration establishes a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per core, prioritizing processing power over memory capacity.

The instance does not feature bundled local storage, meaning all operating system and application data must reside on external network-attached storage volumes. Additionally, the c4n-highcpu-2 does not include integrated GPU hardware or complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures that the instance receives consistent processing performance, avoiding the resource contention and performance degradation associated with shared-core or burstable configurations.

This server type is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads that require dedicated, predictable CPU performance but have minimal memory requirements. It is suited for lightweight compute-focused applications, including microservices, small-scale web serving, containerized application deployments, and basic continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4n-highcpu-2 is a compute-optimized virtual machine belonging to the c4n server family. It features 2 dedicated vCPUs built on the x86_64 CPU architecture, paired with 4.0 GB of system memory, resulting in a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include bundled local storage or GPU accelerators, requiring external storage volumes for data persistence. With its dedicated CPU allocation, the c4n-highcpu-2 provides stable, predictable processing performance without the resource variability of shared-core configurations. It is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads that demand dedicated compute resources but have low memory requirements. Appropriate use cases include lightweight web serving, microservices, containerized application deployments, and basic continuous integration tasks.
